questions.text: What is the effective receptive field size of a stack of three $3 \times 3$ convolutional layers (all with stride 1 and padding 1)? Compare its parameters to a single $7 \times 7$ layer for $C$ channels.
options: Effective receptive field is $7 \times 7$; stacked layers use $27C^2$ parameters versus $49C^2$ parameters for the single $7 \times 7$ layer.
Effective receptive field is $9 \times 9$; stacked layers use $27C^2$ parameters versus $81C^2$ parameters for the single $7 \times 7$ layer.
Effective receptive field is $7 \times 7$; stacked layers use $49C^2$ parameters versus $27C^2$ parameters for the single $7 \times 7$ layer.
Effective receptive field is $5 \times 5$; stacked layers use $18C^2$ parameters versus $49C^2$ parameters for the single $7 \times 7$ layer.
questions.correct_answer: Effective receptive field is $7 \times 7$; stacked layers use $27C^2$ parameters versus $49C^2$ parameters for the single $7 \times 7$ layer.
questions.explanation: Each $3 \times 3$ layer adds 2 pixels to the spatial context window. Stacking three layers yields a receptive field of $1 + (3 - 1) + (3 - 1) + (3 - 1) = 7$. Stacking three layers requires $3 \times (3 \times 3 \times C^2) = 27C^2$ parameters, whereas a single $7 \times 7$ layer requires $7 \times 7 \times C^2 = 49C^2$ parameters, representing a 45% reduction in parameter density.

questions.text: If a residual block input $x$ has shape (64, 28, 28) and the block output $F(x)$ has shape (128, 14, 14), how is the projection shortcut $W_s x$ implemented in practice?
options: Using a $1 \times 1$ convolution with 128 kernels and a stride of 2.
Using a $3 \times 3$ convolution with 128 kernels, stride 1, and zero padding.
Using a max pooling layer of size $2 \times 2$ followed by zero-padding the channels to 128.
Using a bilinear interpolation to downscale resolution, and duplicating channel values.
questions.correct_answer: Using a $1 \times 1$ convolution with 128 kernels and a stride of 2.
questions.explanation: To add $F(x)$ and $x$, their dimensions must match. Since $F(x)$ has halved the spatial size (from 28 to 14) and doubled the channels (from 64 to 128), a $1 \times 1$ convolution with a stride of 2 and 128 kernels projects the input $x$ into the correct shape of (128, 14, 14) with minimal compute cost.
